module_title: Delta Lake and the lakehouse pattern

choices.prompt: According to the module, what is a Delta table actually made of on object storage?
choices: Parquet data files plus a transaction log directory named _delta_log that records which files were added or removed in each commit
A single self-describing ORC file containing both the data and its full version history inline
A relational warehouse table whose rows are streamed out to Parquet only on export
A directory of Parquet files where readers list the folder directly to discover the current valid file set
correct_answers: Parquet data files plus a transaction log directory named _delta_log that records which files were added or removed in each commit

choices.prompt: Northwind's nightly Delta overwrite job crashes after writing only some of its new Parquet files but before appending its commit to the log. What do concurrent dashboard queries see, and why?
choices: The previous complete version of the table, because the new files are not referenced by any commit until the writer atomically appends the commit, leaving the half-written files as ignored orphans
Partial data with no error, exactly as plain Parquet would behave, because the files already exist in the folder
An empty table, because the crash rolled back and deleted both the old and the new Parquet files
A blend of old and new rows, because Delta merges whatever files are present in the directory at read time
correct_answers: The previous complete version of the table, because the new files are not referenced by any commit until the writer atomically appends the commit, leaving the half-written files as ignored orphans

choices.prompt: You restored a 5 TB Delta table to a prior good version and it completed in seconds. Per the module, why can a RESTORE be so fast?
choices: RESTORE only writes a new commit whose valid file set equals the target version's; the underlying Parquet still exists, so it is a metadata operation on the log rather than a data rewrite
RESTORE streams the 5 TB back from a compressed backup snapshot kept by VACUUM
RESTORE rewrites only the partitions that changed, which is usually a small fraction of 5 TB
RESTORE swaps the table to a warehouse replica that already holds the prior version
correct_answers: RESTORE only writes a new commit whose valid file set equals the target version's; the underlying Parquet still exists, so it is a metadata operation on the log rather than a data rewrite

choices.prompt: Based on the module, which statements about Delta Lake time travel are correct?
choices: You can read the table as of a past version or timestamp (e.g. VERSION AS OF 41) as the live table viewed at a past point, with no copy made
Time travel is bounded by a retention window because a VACUUM operation eventually deletes files no longer referenced
Time travel protects against an account deletion or region outage and serves as disaster recovery
Old versions remain queryable forever regardless of any maintenance operation
correct_answers: You can read the table as of a past version or timestamp (e.g. VERSION AS OF 41) as the live table viewed at a past point, with no copy made
Time travel is bounded by a retention window because a VACUUM operation eventually deletes files no longer referenced

choices.prompt: A teammate wants to recover last week's data by copying old Parquet files back into a Delta table's folder with raw file tools. Why does the module warn against this, and what should they do instead?
choices: It bypasses the transaction log so the log no longer matches the files on disk and corrupts the table; instead they should recover through the Delta API using time travel / RESTORE TABLE ... TO VERSION AS OF
It is fine as long as the file names are unique, since Delta auto-detects new Parquet files added to the folder
It is dangerous only because it duplicates storage; instead they should run VACUUM first to clear room before copying
It bypasses schema enforcement; instead they should enable automatic schema merge before copying the files
correct_answers: It bypasses the transaction log so the log no longer matches the files on disk and corrupts the table; instead they should recover through the Delta API using time travel / RESTORE TABLE ... TO VERSION AS OF

choices.prompt: The module describes the problem the lakehouse was created to solve. Before lakehouses, why did teams run a separate data lake and data warehouse, and what was the cost?
choices: The lake gave cheap, scalable, schemaless storage and the warehouse gave transactional governed fast SQL, so data was copied between them, doubling storage and drifting out of sync
The lake handled streaming while the warehouse handled batch, so the same data was never stored in both places and stayed perfectly in sync
The warehouse stored Parquet and the lake stored the transaction log, so neither system could function without the other
Object storage could not hold structured data at all, so the warehouse was mandatory for anything other than raw image and ML files
correct_answers: The lake gave cheap, scalable, schemaless storage and the warehouse gave transactional governed fast SQL, so data was copied between them, doubling storage and drifting out of sync

choices.prompt: According to the module, how does Delta Lake's transaction log enable schema enforcement, and what option does it leave open?
choices: Each commit records the schema, so a write that doesn't match the table's schema is rejected rather than silently corrupting data, while you can opt into controlled schema evolution when you genuinely want to add columns
The log stores only column names, so writes with mismatched data types are silently coerced to the existing types without any rejection
Schema is enforced by VACUUM, which deletes any files whose columns differ from the latest commit during its retention sweep
Delta always auto-merges incoming schemas by default, so production tables widen automatically and enforcement must be turned on per write
correct_answers: Each commit records the schema, so a write that doesn't match the table's schema is rejected rather than silently corrupting data, while you can opt into controlled schema evolution when you genuinely want to add columns

choices.prompt: Based on the module, which statements about Delta's MERGE (upsert) are correct?
choices: MERGE is a single atomic statement that inserts new rows and updates existing ones based on a match condition
MERGE exists because plain Parquet has no update-in-place, and it is the backbone of incremental loading used in later pipelines
Internally MERGE reads the matching files, computes the changed rows, and commits the result as one transaction, so concurrent readers see either the full before or the full after
MERGE applies updates by editing the matched Parquet files in place on disk, so readers may briefly observe a row that is half-updated
correct_answers: MERGE is a single atomic statement that inserts new rows and updates existing ones based on a match condition
MERGE exists because plain Parquet has no update-in-place, and it is the backbone of incremental loading used in later pipelines
Internally MERGE reads the matching files, computes the changed rows, and commits the result as one transaction, so concurrent readers see either the full before or the full after

choices.prompt: The module lists a pitfall about high-write tables, such as streaming or frequent writes. What is the problem and the recommended response?
choices: Many small commits create many small files and a long log, slowly degrading reads; schedule compaction/optimize and log checkpointing for high-write tables
Frequent writes overflow the retention window, so you must lengthen VACUUM retention to avoid losing recent commits
Each write opens a new transaction log directory, so you must periodically delete old _delta_log folders to keep the table fast
Streaming writes bypass the log entirely, so you must replay them through MERGE at the end of each day to make them durable
correct_answers: Many small commits create many small files and a long log, slowly degrading reads; schedule compaction/optimize and log checkpointing for high-write tables

choices.prompt: The module contrasts how readers discover data in a Delta table versus plain Parquet, and warns against one read-time confusion. Which statements are accurate per the module?
choices: Readers never list the data folder and guess; they read the log to learn the current valid set of files
Reading VERSION AS OF does not protect against an account deletion or region outage, because time travel is in-table history, not disaster recovery
Readers determine the current data by listing every Parquet file present in the directory at read time and using whichever ones they find
Time travel replicates the table to another region, so reading a past version doubles as disaster recovery
correct_answers: Readers never list the data folder and guess; they read the log to learn the current valid set of files
Reading VERSION AS OF does not protect against an account deletion or region outage, because time travel is in-table history, not disaster recovery

llm.prompt: Explain how Delta Lake's transaction log turns a multi-file write into an all-or-nothing (ACID) transaction, using Northwind's failed overwrite as the contrast with plain Parquet.
llm.reference_answer: With plain Parquet a multi-file write is not atomic: Northwind's writer deleted the old files and wrote new ones directly, so a crash left the folder in a broken in-between state and queries returned partial data with no error for hours. With Delta, the writer never touches the reader's view until it is done: it first writes the new Parquet files, then atomically appends a single JSON commit to the _delta_log saying 'remove old files A, B, C; add new files X, Y, Z.' Until that one commit lands, readers continue to see the old, complete version; if the job crashes before committing, the half-written files are never referenced by any log entry and are ignored as orphans. Because readers learn the current valid set of files from the log rather than listing the folder, the table is always in a consistent state, and that single atomic log append is what makes the multi-file write all-or-nothing.

llm.prompt: Walk through how you would use MERGE to upsert a batch of corrected sensor readings into the Delta silver table, and explain what concurrent readers see during the operation.
llm.reference_answer: Load the corrections batch, get a DeltaTable handle for the silver path, and call merge with a match condition on the natural key (for example t.device_id = s.device_id AND t.event_date = s.event_date). Use whenMatchedUpdate to fix existing rows (e.g. set moisture to the source value) and whenNotMatchedInsertAll to insert new devices, then execute. MERGE is a single atomic statement that inserts new rows and updates existing ones based on the match condition — an upsert — which Delta does because plain Parquet has no update-in-place. Internally it reads the matching files, computes the changed rows, and commits the result as one transaction, so concurrent readers see either the full before state or the full after state, never a partial result. This atomic upsert is the backbone of incremental loading used in later pipelines.

llm.prompt: Compare time travel and RESTORE for recovering from a bad load, and explain why the module insists time travel is not a substitute for a real backup or disaster recovery.
llm.reference_answer: Time travel lets you read the table as of any past version or timestamp (e.g. VERSION AS OF 41), viewing the exact file set valid at that commit as the live table with no copy made — useful for debugging, reproducible reports, and auditing. RESTORE builds on the same mechanism to recover: if a bad job corrupts version 42, you RESTORE to version 41, which writes a new commit (version 43) whose valid file set equals version 41's; no Parquet is rewritten, so even a 5 TB restore finishes in seconds because only the log changes. However, time travel is in-table history bounded by a retention window: a VACUUM operation eventually removes files no longer referenced beyond that window, breaking time travel to those versions. It also does nothing against an account deletion or region outage, so it is not disaster recovery — for compliance and DR you must back up deliberately rather than rely on time travel.
